Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: The user wants a DuckDB SQL query that filters out certain cases based on a sequence of activities and a time condition The final result should include all events except those from the cases that meet both criteria

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: First I need to identify the cases that have the exact sequence of Approve Purchase Requisition followed by Create Order and then Confirm Order in immediate succession That means no other events from the same case can be between these three So for each case I need to check the order of activities and ensure that these three appear consecutively in that exact order

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Next among those cases I need to check if the time between Create Order and Confirm Order in that sequence is more than 5 days If both the sequence and timing conditions are met the case should be excluded from the final result

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: So the plan is 1 Assign a row number to each event within a case ordered by timestamp This will help in checking the sequence of activities 2 Use window functions to look for the next two activities in the sequence to ensure they follow immediately after the first activity in the sequence 3 Calculate the time difference between the Create Order and Confirm Order events for the identified sequences 4 Mark the cases that meet both conditions sequence and timing and then exclude all their events from the final result

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Wait but how do I ensure that the three activities are consecutive Let us think For each occurrence of Approve Purchase Requisition I need to check if the next activity by timestamp is Create Order and the one after that is Confirm Order So using the row number partitioned by case_id and ordered by timestamp I can check the next two rows

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Alternatively using the LEAD window function For each row check the next two activities If the current activity is Approve Purchase Requisition the next LEAD 1 should be Create Order and the next after that LEAD 2 should be Confirm Order That way the three form a consecutive sequence

Name: Validation - PE
Text: But wait what if there are other activities in between For example if after Approve Purchase Requisition there is another activity before Create Order Then the LEAD approach would not catch that So the LEAD function would check the next two rows in order which are the immediate next events So if the three activities are consecutive their row numbers would be consecutive So the LEAD approach should work here

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: So step 1 For each event in the event_log assign a row number within the case ordered by timestamp Then for rows where activity is Approve Purchase Requisition check if the next row activity is Create Order and the third row activity is Confirm Order

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: But how to structure this in SQL Let us think Using a common table expression CTE to add row numbers and lead activities

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Then in the next step for each case check if there exists a trio of consecutive rows where the activities are in the required order So for each row in ordered_events where activity is Approve Purchase Requisition we need to check if the next two rows rn plus 1 and rn plus 2 exist and have the required activities

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Another approach For each row check if the current activity is Approve Purchase Requisition the next activity using LEAD is Create Order and the next next activity LEAD 1 2 is Confirm Order But that would require using LEAD twice

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Then for rows where activity is Approve Purchase Requisition and next_activity1 is Create Order and next_activity2 is Confirm Order those rows are part of the sequence But we need to capture the entire case if such a sequence exists in any part of the case

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: In the sequence Create Order is the next activity after Approve and Confirm Order is the third So the time between Create Order and Confirm Order is the difference between their timestamps

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: For each row in the ordered_events if the current activity is Approve and the next is Create and the one after is Confirm then the time difference between Create and Confirm is the timestamp of the third row rn plus 2 minus the timestamp of the second row rn plus 1

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: So perhaps with ordered_events as select case_id activity timestamp lead activity 1 over partition by case_id order by timestamp as next_act1 lead activity 2 over partition by case_id order by timestamp as next_act2 lead timestamp 1 over partition by case_id order by timestamp as next_ts1 lead timestamp 2 over partition by case_id order by timestamp as next_ts2 from event_log

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: The next step is to find all case_ids that have at least one occurrence of this sequence where the time between Create Order and Confirm Order is more than 5 days Once we have those case_ids we need to exclude all events from those cases from the final result

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: So the steps are 1 Identify all case_ids that have the sequence and the time condition 2 Select all events from event_log where the case_id is not in the identified list

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: First create a CTE that identifies the sequences and checks the time difference Then collect the case_ids from there Then in the main query select all events where the case_id is not in that list

Name: Validation - PE
Text: In the problem_sequences CTE we are selecting case_ids where there is at least one occurrence of the sequence with the time condition Then the main query selects all events not in those cases

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Need to make sure that the time difference is calculated correctly The difference between next_ts2 timestamp of Confirm Order and next_ts1 timestamp of Create Order is indeed the duration between Create and Confirm

Name: Validation - PE
Text: What about cases where there are multiple sequences For example a case might have multiple instances of the sequence But we only need to exclude the case once So the EXISTS clause in the main query will check if the case_id is present in the problem_sequences list regardless of how many times it occurs

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Another edge case a case has the correct sequence but between Create Order and Confirm Order there is another event But in our approach since we are checking the next two activities after Approve if there is an event in between the next_act1 would not be Create Order so the sequence would not be detected So that is correct because the problem requires the three activities to be in direct succession

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The LEAD approach correctly captures consecutive events

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Wait but in the ordered_events CTE do we need the ROW_NUMBER Because in the LEAD function the order is determined by the partition and order by So even without the ROW_NUMBER the LEAD functions would work as they are ordered by timestamp So perhaps the ROW_NUMBER is not necessary

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: The ordered_events CTE includes ROW_NUMBER but it is not used So perhaps the ordered_events CTE can be simplified

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Let me adjust with with_leads as select case_id activity timestamp lead activity 1 over partition by case_id order by timestamp as next_act1 lead activity 2 over partition by case_id order by timestamp as next_act2 lead timestamp 1 over partition by case_id order by timestamp as next_ts1 lead timestamp 2 over partition by case_id order by timestamp as next_ts2 from event_log problem_sequences as select case_id from with_leads where activity equals Approve Purchase Requisition and next_act1 equals Create Order and next_act2 equals Confirm Order and next_ts2 minus next_ts1 greater than interval 5 days select all from event_log where case_id not in select case_id from problem_sequences

Name: Validation - PE
Text: The problem_sequences would include the case_id once so the not in clause would exclude all events from that case regardless That is correct as per the problem statement exclude the entire case if it meets the criteria

Name: Validation - PE
Text: What if a case has the sequence but the time difference is exactly 5 days Then it is not included in problem_sequences so the case is not excluded That is correct

Name: Validation - PE

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Another possible approach is to use a self join For each case find the Approve event then check if there is a Create event after it and a Confirm event after that However that might be more complex especially ensuring that the events are consecutive

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: The window function approach seems more straightforward here

Name: Validation - PE
Text: DuckDB handling of intervals The subtraction of next_ts2 minus next_ts1 gives an interval result Comparing that to interval 5 days should work So next_ts2 minus next_ts1 greater than interval 5 days is correct

Name: Validation - PE
